coredata.dc:title: Advancements in performance of zinc oxide/carbon quantum dots based photovoltaic trigeneration system using genetic algorithm and particle swarm optimization
coredata.dc:creator: Tyagi, Sakshi
Kumar Singh, Pawan
Kumar Tiwari, Arun
coredata.dc:description: Correct parametric extraction is vital for efficient photovoltaic cell designing and study of concerned system attributes, but the considerable nonlinearity of the results from the current–voltage curve makes this a difficult task. Consequently, in recent years, various interesting meta-heuristic techniques have been proposed to further expedite this development due to rapid advances in computer technology and swarm intelligence. In this work, two meta-heuristic methods, i.e., Genetic Algorithm and the Particle Swarm Optimisation method are used to identify the photovoltaic parameters of Zinc Oxide and Carbon Quantum Dots based hybrid cells. The values of optimised parameters, i.e., photo generated current, saturation current, series resistance, shunt resistance, using Genetic Algorithm are 0.567 mA, 0.077 mA, 61.282 Ω, 1.203 Ω respectively, and using Particle Swarm Optimization are 0.636 mA, 0.079 mA, 58.367 Ω, 0.975 Ω respectively. These results were validated using a highly potent optimization method, i.e., Artificial Neural Network using the firefly algorithm, the predicted photovoltaic characteristics aided in superior energy management and the proper operation of the photovoltaic system. The optimised results are deployed in a solar trigeneration model which is used in a commercial building. To substantiate the application area, a simulation tool, Polysun 12.0.8, is utilised to simulate and analyse the performance of hybrid cells. It is understood that the cell optimised using Particle Swarm Optimization obtains 7.85 % higher efficacy than the one optimised using Genetic Algorithm. The ultimate aim is to improve the efficiency of the solar systems and cope with the energy requirements by providing sufficient power, cooling and heating energy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid solar cell
Zinc oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Genetic algorithms
Particle swarm optimization
Photovoltaic trigeneration systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of PV parameters of source model using metaheuristic approach
Implementation of study in solar trigeneration sections

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemical Engineering, Universiti Teknologi Petronas

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their special gratitude to Mr. Shobhit Singh, Deputy Manager, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, Purba Medinipur, West Bengal, India for his extensive help in performing our study on solar trigeneration system at the North service centre building of HPL, India. Also, we would like to acknowledge Dr. Radha Das, Professor and Dean, Department of Chemical Engineering, Haldia Institute of Technology and Dr. Rabindra N. Jana, Associate Professor, Department of Chemical Engineering, Haldia Institute of Technology, for their constant support provided during the experimental research phase.

head.ce:title: Advancements in performance of zinc oxide/carbon quantum dots based photovoltaic trigeneration system using genetic algorithm and particle swarm optimization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Institute of Engineering and Technology, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh 226021, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Institute of Engineering and Technology, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh 226021, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Correct parametric extraction is vital for efficient photovoltaic cell designing and study of concerned system attributes, but the considerable nonlinearity of the results from the current–voltage curve makes this a difficult task. Consequently, in recent years, various interesting meta-heuristic techniques have been proposed to further expedite this development due to rapid advances in computer technology and swarm intelligence. In this work, two meta-heuristic methods, i.e., Genetic Algorithm and the Particle Swarm Optimisation method are used to identify the photovoltaic parameters of Zinc Oxide and Carbon Quantum Dots based hybrid cells. The values of optimised parameters, i.e., photo generated current, saturation current, series resistance, shunt resistance, using Genetic Algorithm are 0.567 mA, 0.077 mA, 61.282 Ω, 1.203 Ω respectively, and using Particle Swarm Optimization are 0.636 mA, 0.079 mA, 58.367 Ω, 0.975 Ω respectively. These results were validated using a highly potent optimization method, i.e., Artificial Neural Network using the firefly algorithm, the predicted photovoltaic characteristics aided in superior energy management and the proper operation of the photovoltaic system. The optimised results are deployed in a solar trigeneration model which is used in a commercial building. To substantiate the application area, a simulation tool, Polysun 12.0.8, is utilised to simulate and analyse the performance of hybrid cells. It is understood that the cell optimised using Particle Swarm Optimization obtains 7.85 % higher efficacy than the one optimised using Genetic Algorithm. The ultimate aim is to improve the efficiency of the solar systems and cope with the energy requirements by providing sufficient power, cooling and heating energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:def-description.ce:para: social parameter in particle swarm optimization algorithm
cognitive parameter in particle swarm optimization algorithm
total global solar irradiance on the surface at tilted angle, kWh
speed of particle in genetic algorithm

ce:section.ce:para: The exhaustion of fossil fuels, along with a considerable increase in energy demands and other environmental issues, are the major reasons for many countries to prioritize the evolution of renewable energy resources. Nowadays, sustainable energy is one of the vital resolutions to meet the power demands of the upcoming era. It can be used as an eco-efficient substitute to fossil fuels and possesses enormous benefits due to being clean, green, and toxin-free. Solar energy is assumed to be the best sustainable energy source and has proved to be a marvelous substitute to fossil fuels because of its low cost, easy availability and environmentally friendly nature. The hybrid cells are prepared by the union of inorganic and organic semiconductors. Here the cell is formed by merging ZnO and CQDs. Being an n-type material, ZnO is suitable for application in photovoltaic (PV) devices [1,2]. It is exceptionally superior in optical, electrical, and constructional properties. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are a good option in the nano carbon discipline because of their application in the field of electronics [3,4]. Table 1 shows the efficiency variations, advantages and disadvantages of different types of photovoltaic cells over the years [4].

For studying and forecasting the definite properties of PV systems, precise PV cell modeling is quite vital. There have been many developments, including the single diode model (SDM), improved single diode model (ISDM), double diode model (DDM), triple diode model, modified double diode model (MDDM), and many more [5]. However, in practice, the aforementioned designs struggle to maintain consistent and satisfactory execution. Furthermore, the manufacturer's I-V curve is only created under prescribed test settings (STC). External environmental changes, like sun irradiance and temperature, have a significant impact on PV cell output characteristics. Furthermore, PV cells have inherent properties such as significant nonlinearity of PV characteristics. In particular, efficient parameter identification is at the heart of exact PV cell modeling. Parameter identification is also critical for PV system designing, functionality assessment, optimal results, and concurrent regulation [6]. It can also help with battery fabrication, photoelectric conversion, and maximum power point tracking (MPPT) [7]. Since the importance of parameter extraction has aroused intellectual concerns, a plethora of studies have been conducted in order to find feasible solutions. As a result, many approaches to cracking this tough nut have been proposed [8], with these approaches largely falling into two categories [9], namely analytical methods [10] and meta-heuristic algorithms [11]. To extract these parameters, the analytical approaches use a sequence of sophisticated mathematical equations. Reference [12] used a combination of statistical and analytical methodologies to successfully find the unknown values. However, such systems have a number of drawbacks, including a high calculation burden and complex mathematical computations that take a long time and cost a lot of money [12]. Various meta-heuristic methods, on the other hand, have found extensive applications with the advent of computer intellect [13]. The major notable benefit is that they do not require a detailed mathematical model of the researched system [14], reducing the computing stress significantly. There have been a slew of meta-heuristic approaches proclaimed for identifying PV cell parameters at breakneck speeds. Table 1 represents advancements achieved in the domain of photovoltaic over the years [15,40]. It explains the revolutionary change in the application area of solar energy where a comparative analysis of photoelectric conversion energy, advantages and disadvantages of mono-crystalline silicon cells, poly-crystalline silicon cells, thin film cells, and hybrid photovoltaic cells is presented.

The simulation model and method to regulate the cell parameters should be chosen wisely so as to achieve precise evaluation of the PV system, better reliability, good quality, and performance of solar cells and systems [16]. The idea is to develop a GA and PSO model for ZnO/CQD hybrid solar cells by extracting data from experimental results and literature which has been used for enhancing the performance of hybrid solar cells made of zinc oxide, graphene oxide, titanium dioxide, and carbon quantum dot incorporated nickel oxide. The current study takes a lead from previous research and aims to portray the resultant output obtained by implementation of the two metaheuristic approaches on a different version of hybrid cell made of ZnO/CQDs [17]. Table 2 shows a description of recent research explaining how metaheuristic techniques (biological and physical) are used for estimation of PV cell variables [18,19]. It explains the advantages and disadvantages of the chosen genetic algorithm and particle swarm optimisation technique, stating the application of the same in predicting the parametric results of single diode and dual diode cell models [24]. The type of data used in optimization is also listed, i.e., whether acquired experimentally or by the manufacturer of the cell. A literature survey of recent years is structured so as to check the efficacy of the chosen techniques on the different types of cell models. This helped in suggesting the novel idea to commence the presented investigation.

Application of GA and PSO has been accomplished to analyze various specifications of PV cells. The analysis focuses on forecasting the suitable system for a fabricated cell. The efficacy of the two algorithms will be checked with a highly potent Artificial Neural Network using the firefly algorithm. This work is additionally employed in various land forms, subjected to the spectral power density and temperature of the location. As a result, the required cells can be designed to match the demands based on the specific installation site. The novelty of this work lies in the use of different metaheuristic approaches to predict the device parameters of ZnO/CQDs cells, hence delivering the best algorithm to suit the energy demands and proving to be energy efficient [20]. The discipline of solar energy is a vast pool of resources where one can find different opportunities for applications. One such application is solar trigeneration and polygeneration units. Solar trigeneration systems are highly efficient in power generation, along with heating and cooling of the area, running concurrently with an electric refrigeration unit [21]. The suggested enactment of the hybrid solar cell is applied in the PV trigeneration model installed at the South Service Centre (Polymer building) of Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, West Bengal. The recommended unit design incorporates a self-operated solar electric system, a solar DHW system which runs on solar energy, and a heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning (HVAC) unit. The formulated models are advantageous in determining system performance based on the obtained results from GA and PSO. Hence, identification of a better system is achieved, which is cost-effective and eco-friendly at the same time. This study aims at examining the features of PV modules, PV/T collectors, and heat pumps, which blend together to make an efficient PV trigeneration system for the commercial arena [22,23]. The discussed methodology in this work states the design of a system which is potential enough to produce electricity for the uninterrupted operation of an off-grid commercial and housing complex. This research can be further expanded in other application areas, which may depend on the suggested need. Literature reveals that many models exhibit the materialistic attributes of a PV cell, which is capable of replicating the current–voltage (I-V) curve in an actual environment. The commonly used models are single (one) diode and dual (two) diode-based equivalent circuit models [24]. In this research work, we have used a dual diode equivalent circuit model. Then two metaheuristic techniques are applied to recognize the device metrics. Discussion is given on the mathematical model used for the simulative investigation and parametric identification of hybrid cells. A detailed analysis of the theoretical and experimental results is performed, followed by a strong conclusion. Fig. 1 explains the research objective for the presented study. There has been a few research documented in the literature on this pair of cells utilizing metaheuristic methodologies, and the distinguishing achievement of this approach is its implementation in a solar trigeneration system, which opens up new avenues for future research in the solar field ().

Numerous techniques have been evolved for hybrid cell parametric identification; they can be categorized according to the data sets used for the extraction of parameters. Extraction methods can be distinguished according to the following [21]:
•
Data granted by the manufacturer of the PV module (results under standard conditions).
•
Experimental device metrics.
Another category depends on the extraction technique chosen, i.e. Methodical, Numeral and Meta-heuristic methods based results [22].
a) Methodical results

These types of solutions basically use the I-V attributes and the features provided by the manufacturer at the standard test conditions. The unknown parameters are evaluated on the basis of clear mathematical analysis [23,24]. However, the accuracy of the measured values are sole reason for the effectiveness of the techniques [24], the drawback with this model is that without exact metrics the systematic model cannot link up to a practical model [25].
b) Numeral results:

To identify and evaluate the parameters, numerical techniques use complete or a part of the measured data values [25,26]. To estimate the values of ideality factors and resistors, iterative algorithms are used, such as the Newton-Raphson, recursive least squares, and Levenberg-Marquardt algorithms. But they robustly depend on the primary parameters, so insignificant solutions are obtained. Also, specified requirements must be validated, such as convexity and differentiability.
(c) Meta-heuristic methods based results:

In the current literature, research has been conducted on the application of metaheuristic techniques for the recognition of PV cell parameters. Both evolutionary computation and stochastic optimization have the capacity to deal with nonlinear and multi-model problems, which could be efficiently used for the extraction of the PV cell parameters.

Numerous methods, such as simulated annealing (SA) [20], Bird Mating Optimizer (BMO) [20], Genetic Algorithms (GA) [20,21], Differential Evolution (DE) [20,21], Harmony Search Algorithm (IGHS) [20,21], Pattern Search (PS) [22], and finally Artificial Bee Swarm Optimization (ABSO) [22], have been presented in the literature.

To explain the exceptional nonlinear behavior of a PV cell, there are many mathematical models which deal with the nonlinearity present at the semiconductor junctions. According to the number of junctions, the models are based on single or twin diodes [23].

The single diode model is a clarified depiction of the PV cell, which is extracted from the Shockley diode equation by introducing a parameter Aq [23]. Fig. 2(a), b
represents the wiring diagrams of one diode and the dual diode model of the hybrid cell. Also, Table 3 gives a brief description of the comparative study made between single and dual diode models based on a literature survey, which enlists their benefits, drawbacks, and most important findings [25].

The following equation (1) shows the basic operation of the one diode model [24,25].
(1)

In single diode circuit, the shunt resistance is negligible. The model can be stated using the equation (2) [23].
(2)

Dual diode representation

The parameter AO is presented here to consider the non-linearity of the PN junction of the hybrid solar cell. Refer to equation (3). Shunt resistance can be considered negligible because of its low value [23,25]. Table 4 summarizes all of the characteristics that must be determined for hybrid solar cells [26].
(3)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PV parameters of source model using metaheuristic approach
ce:section.ce:para: The PV cell behaviour is reproduced by the I-V curve, which is based on the dual model results obtained from equation (1), with a constant solar irradiance of 1000 W/m2 and a temperature of 25°. After attaining this curve, identification methods will be used to find cell parameters [23,24]. The identified system parameters and the range of search for each parameter are listed below.

The identification error is denoted by fe in equation (4). It is used to compute the variation between the currents of the PV cell, i.e., iactual (real) and ievaluated (estimated). The following equation is used to represent the identification error: The cost function is minimized by this error [23,25].
(4)
f e
=
∑
1
N
(
i
actual
-
i
evaluated
)
2
Here, two meta-heuristic algorithms, i.e., Genetic Algorithm and Particle Swarm Optimization, are presented to identify the PV cell parameters.

The elementary idea behind the implementation of GA is to determine an individual’s population, where every individual is considered to be a collection of variables, and to trace out the worthy individuals that are capable of minimizing the cost function represented in equation (4). The process is repeated with each new population set until the error reaches a predetermined point [4,20]. A genetic algorithm (GA) is well-versed in solving problems that are a combination of continuous distinct variables with a non-uniform and non-convex design space; it provides the best solution possible. Darwin suggested this algorithm and presented the theory of survival of the fittest [7]. The first transformation by GA converts the basic constrained function to an unrestricted function by the application of the penalty function, represented in equations (5),6,7,8 [27].
(5)
z
1
= 155.9 - 0.1540 x
1
- 0.506
x
2
+
0.000502 x
1
x
2
;
(6)
z
2
= 126 - 0.125 x
1
- 0.412
x
2
+
0.000409
x
1

2
;
(7)
z
3
=
- 2869 + 2.9
x

+ 9.6

;
(8)
z
4
= - 222 + 0.222
x
1
+ 0.733
x
2
- 0.000729
x
1
x
2
;

Fig. 3 shows the flowchart which explains the various optimization processes for a dual diode model. The information on adaptation, generation, and variance operations is referred to in this study [26].

Particle swarm optimization (PSO) is defined as an optimization method inspirited by the societal efforts of organic structures, for example, with the help of a combination of expressions, the birds group together to find a food source. This is influenced by the present location of the body through the previous and adjoining locations. Nonlinear equations can be easily solved by this algorithm [7].

The primary concept is to create a group of particles and further to process them depending upon definite measure to acquire the objective [28]. The particle motion can be expressed as follows in equation (9) and (10) [27,28].
(9)
X j
t + 1
=
X j
t
+
V j
t + 1
(10)
V j
t + 1
= w
V j
t
+
C 1
r 1
p i
-
X j
t
+
C 2
r 2
p g
-
X j
t

If considering individual particles Xj, the speed Vj is determined as a linear blend of three elements, which are its deviation from its pi neighborhood, its own speed, and finally a comparison is made to the finest pg particle. Then, the present orientation of the particle is rationalized through the coefficients C1, C2 and w to achieve the advanced location. The randomness of the last two coefficients (r1 and r2) can be easily measured by this method [28,29].

The ability of the presented PSO and GA algorithms to obtain an optimal neural network model for evaluating key photovoltaic parameters is investigated in this research. The ANN model was acquired using experimental data from the investigation of the hybrid cell in the hot and humid climate area of Haldia, West Bengal, India. Table 11 shows the satisfactory data for input and output parameters (in the appendix). To extrapolate the experiment, different percentages of data were used during learning, assessment, and verification. We may conclude that for this form of data collection, any feasible combination will yield a good outcome for this study. Table 5 shows the proportion of data distribution for the solar cell under the three groupings. The data is then normalised, which constitutes one of the most crucial processes in training the ANN model for the following reasons: The first and most essential point is that all entries must be assured that they have the same weight because the algorithm employs distance-based constraints. In other words, if the input ranges are different, the larger one will be preferred during training. The second issue is that, because of its restricted domain, the transfer function employed in the convolution layer may only be performed across a restricted range of values.

First and foremost, the aim is intended to verify the efficacy of the suggested GA and PSO methods by comparing them to an extremely potent algorithm, namely ANN-FA, so as to prove the suggested algorithm's competence for training the ANN model. In this context, a brief description of the validation algorithm is provided. The mediaeval meta-heuristic firefly algorithm is described to determine the various parameters of the solar cell. The performance index of a given optimization model is associated with the flashlight in the firefly algorithm [17,41]. Furthermore, it is believed that the luminous intensity aids the colony of flies in travelling towards brighter and more fascinating areas in order to find the best outcomes for the solar variables. The ANN is trained in such a way that the discrepancy between the model parameters and the desired outcome is reduced. The weights and biases are then adjusted to minimise errors and obtain the greatest possible benefit from the model outputs. Dynamic updating of biases and weights results in a trustworthy system with the minimum amount of error. The firefly algorithm has been used here by employing the utility function approach or the weighted function method. It is a methodical approach to resolving conflicts arising from diverse agendas.

After acquiring experimental data for the crucial characteristics and comparing them to previously reported work, the prepared photovoltaic cell was reproduced by simulation experiments for different temperatures and spectral densities, and thus Iph, Isat, Rp, andRs of the cell, as well as the I-V characteristics, were recorded. In total, 8571 iterations were performed, yielding 97,157 values that were leveraged to train the model for building the dataset [16,17]. Tables 7 and 8
show the parameter settings and responses for the experimental runs of ZnO/CQDs solar cells, which were recorded during a 16-day period. Furthermore, for the four output responses, this dataset was separated into certain proportions of training, testing, and validation in order to extract the highest value of output from the modelled ANN. To configure the data, all input model parameters were adjusted, and the voltage and current values were reported correspondingly. Using MATLAB (R2015a) software, a multilayered feed-forward neural network with one convolution layer (10 neurons) was programmed, employing the firefly algorithm (system memory: 16 GB). Following training, the network's results for each photometric parameter were provided, as shown in Figs. 4 and 5
for GA results and PSO results, respectively. Also, these figures depict the coefficient of correlation (R) value during training, validation, and testing, as well as the overall output result, i.e., photo generated current (Iph) obtained using GA and PSO, respectively. The statistics show that the path goes linearly across about 90 % of the overall data and illustrates a clear association between the points, resulting in an acceptable value of R for all ZnO/CQDs cell characteristics [31]. Finally, to validate the outcomes of the GA and PSO approaches, the standard algorithms ANN-FA are trained independently rather than during the ANN prediction process, as shown in Fig. 6 . Furthermore, the ideal results in these models are obtained by using the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) as a fitness value. To accomplish this, Table 5 displays values for two techniques used during deep learning, with the tangential sigmoid in the convolution layer and purely in the output layer serving as the converter. It also includes RMSE and R2 numbers to help understand the strategies. It is certain that both models have high predictive accuracy to display the outputs with the highest correlation coefficient values (R2). Subsequently, Fig. 6 represents optimised results using ANN-FA and the values of R2 for the initial and subsequent predictions, which are 0.997 and 0.996, respectively, indicate the validity of PSO and GA. Based on the observations, it is also clear that PSO is a strong analyzer.

In a nutshell, the regression model's score is close to one, indicating that it is designed to produce good results and that a relevant correlation has been formed between process variables and the predicted value [30,32].

Further, to substantiate the analysis, the efficacy of the two presented techniques is examined; the strength of 50 individuals is selected for the GA technique; the solution is extracted after 100 generations. It can be observed that the actual result goes along in perfect consensus with the identified results, as shown in Fig. 7 . Likewise, for the PSO technique, the strength of 100 individuals is chosen and the final calculations are performed after 100 generations, as shown in Fig. 8 . In the PSO approach too, actual and estimated results are compared and convergence is achieved accordingly.

Figs. 7 and 8 also show the actual and estimated I-V characteristics are quite similar. Also, the relative error is within the scope [-12 × 10-4; 4 × 10-4] for the GA technique and [-3 × 10-17; 16 × 10-17] for the PSO technique. In accordance with Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), the value obtained from the two algorithms differs in the time (speed) of convergence to the global optimum. Table 6 represents the population size and iteration distribution for both the metaheuristic techniques.

From the estimated values, it is deduced that both the optimization methods, i.e., GA and PSO, have good universal exploration ability. The intersection speed of PSO is swift for the extracted parameters. Choosing the best of C1 and C2 parameters in the PSO algorithm is vital for the algorithm's convergence. To enhance the results, it was required to foster cognitive parameters (C2 = 1) with respect to social setting (C1 = 3) [18,33].

As a result, the ideal values of learning factors C1 and C2 are determined using a risk assessment of these coefficients. Different C1 and C2 configurations are used in GA and PSO algorithms, and the results for training and testing data sets based on R2 and RMSE are shown in Table 7. As is evident from the data, version no. 7 (C1 = 1.42 and C2 = 2.37) outperforms the other models. As a result, these values were chosen as the best learning model parameters in this investigation. Following that, the analysis of possible factors impacting mutations and crossovers in the PSO-GA algorithm, as shown in Table 10 , reveals the optimum values for these variables [34].

For investigational analysis of the ZnO/CQDs cell, I-V curve tracer and portable peak power measuring device (PVPM 1000C) is used to generate 100 points on I-V curve, as shown in Fig. 9(a),(b) ,. Table 8 shows the search ranges for the two algorithms.

Difficulties were encountered to maintain the standard conditions of 25° and a solar irradiance of 1000 W/m2. Hence, the listed metrics of the dual diode PV model in equation (1) can be indicated by this modified version of equation (11) and (12) [35].
I
ph
=
I
ph , r e f
- ( 1 + k
o
( T - 300 )
)

( -
k 4
T )

Iph,ref, Rs ref, and RP ref represent the parametric values in standard conditions, whereas k0 and k4 are coefficients associated with the type of solar cell. Also, Vbi is the PN junction's potential basis. Table 8 recapitulates the determined variables for both techniques. The unknown parameters are identified from the search range determined from the I-V curve. RMSE is calculated by using the hit and miss method. The determined and actual I-V curves show a close relationship to each other, especially in the area representing the maximum value of power. The parameters observed during the GA calculation are presented in the appendix. A solution is pareto-optimal in decision variable space if it is not dominated by any other solution. The obtained solution is the best known (optimal) answer for all objectives, and it can't be improved in one without deteriorating in another [31]. This non-dominant collection is the group of all possible results that are not dominated by any other solution. It is called a “globally optimum set” if the non-dominant collection is contained within the whole viable space for investigation [36].

For optimization, the total number of necessary iterations is 182, which results in 80 combinations used as control variables and feedback. Table 8 illustrates the related output results. As soon as the tolerance value is reached for the pareto solution, optimization reaches the termination stage. Four parameters are optimized simultaneously and are represented in different plot functions of GA. It is evident from Figs. 7 and 8 that the feedback parameters change within a range. For Iph,the range is 0.567 mA to 0.583 mA. Is ranges from 0.0778 mA to 0.0888 mA, RP ranges from 61.28 Ω to 61.79 Ω and RS ranges from 1.203 Ω to 1.227 Ω.

A considerable amount of research has been conducted to optimize several performance parameters of photovoltaic systems in different contexts with the efficient use of ANN. Silicon based tandem solar cells were optimized by Chaudhary et al. [22], where they optimized silicon tandem solar cells using artificial neural networks. A network with 40 hidden neurons was developed in MATLAB and trained using the Bayesian regularization algorithm. This trained network was used to predict values of input parameters to generate the most optimum model of solar cell. Bae et al. [24,25] applied ANN-based modeling for estimating photovoltaic properties of silicon-based solar cells. They were able to improve the properties of cells using modeling and simulation. Using the previous studies, a comparison has been made between the obtained results and hypothetical work. The comparative results are shown in Tables 9 and 10, which elaborate on RMSE and R2 values for GA, PSO, and ANN-FA for ZnO/CQDs and silicon-based cells, respectively. Figs. 10 and 11
show absolute residual errors obtained from GA, PSO, and ANN-FA for ZnO/CQDs and silicon-based cells, respectively. It is clear from the figures that errors encountered in the PSO technique are fewer as compared to the other two. Hence, the PSO method outperforms in the validation stage for ZnO/CQDs cells, proving the hybrid material better than the conventional silicon cells. Additionally, Figs. 12, 13, and 14
shows training, testing, validation, and overall coefficient of correlation curves for silicon-based cells, using GA, PSO, and ANN-FA [20,25].

To commence with the interpretations, the ANN was run 50 times using each of the four training set dimensions listed in Table 6 to determine the optimal training set size. Based on the results, the appropriate size of the test and training sets was determined to be 70 % and 30 %, respectively, based on their minimum mean RMSE values. In other words, the previously indicated training and validation sets with RSME values of 0.3315 and 0.3528 for the PSO and GA, respectively, were selected to achieve a more precise and reliable assessment model. Table 7 displays the outcomes of various cognitive factors (C1 and C2) on the ANN model's performance. Furthermore, plots of the training, assessment, verification, and overall coefficient of correlation (R) for GA and PSO findings are shown in Figs. 4 and 5. Furthermore, the effects of different population sizes on PSO and GA performance are explored. Fig. 9 (a) and (b) depict impact analysis for this parameter. It is discovered that the amount of RMSE decreases with increasing population size, making it harder to select the ideal network model. As shown in Figs. 7 and 8, population size 100 yields the best RMSE statistics for both outputs substantially in all iterations. As a result, this size was incorporated into the PSO model for further analysis. Fig. 15 also depicts the GA simulated feedback parameters of the provided PV hybrid solar cell.

As a result, these values were chosen as the best learning factor values in this investigation. Following that, the analysis of various factors influencing the crossover and mutation of the PSO-GA algorithm, as shown in Table 9, reveals that the optimum values for these variables are the same as previously examined. In the next steps, the ANN-FA model's performance was contrasted to that of the PSO and GA algorithms [22,25]. The absolute residual error of the dataset between the actual and output models for 14 samples is clearly illustrated in Fig. 10. The findings demonstrate that the deviation range (0.000461–0.0453) of the predicted values generated from the proposed method for the ZnO/CQDs cell is smaller than that of the GA (0.003674–0.1128) and ANN-FA (0.000344–0.11321) for the same. Furthermore, the variation interval of the silicon-based cell employing PSO to predict the output was (0.0028–0.0472), which gives superior results than GA (0.003717–0.01209) and the ANN-FA (0.003474–0.0102954). A comparison of the suggested model with ANN-FA yields the optimum correlation coefficient (R2) for ZnO/CQDs and silicon-based models, respectively [41]. These figures show that the PSO can outperform the GA and ANN-FA predictor models in terms of prediction accuracy, with 0.997 R2 values. Overall, despite the fact that both intelligence algorithms forecast with great accuracy, the PSO model can be implemented when more precise findings are necessary.

The following are the key contributions/findings:
a)
Each algorithm's concept, key benefits, and drawbacks for PV cell experimental validation are discussed, with an emphasis on application accuracy, convergence speed, and practicability. When compared to prior studies in this sector, this study has significantly improved the diversity of algorithms, simulated results, and comparison of multiple techniques. As a result of these investigations, updated and customised variants can be offered to substantially upgrade existing approaches.
b)
The advantages of various methods for detecting PV cell attributes, such as Simulated Annealing, which is essentially unaffected by variations in irradiation level, must be fully utilized. Several new hybrid algorithms have been proposed and implemented to attain more favourable results by effectively incorporating and employing the merits of various approaches;
c)
It is worth noting that the PV cell variables provided by manufacturers or experimental studies are generally tested under STC, whereas workable process conditions can barely maintain STC. As a consequence, in order to verify the realistic performance of the proposed algorithms, investigations must be conducted under a multitude of operating situations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implementation of study in solar trigeneration sections

ce:section.ce:para: All trigeneration systems are based on three energy outputs having distinct thermal qualities that are preferable with the system, which makes it a source of energy for steam generation of varied qualities like dry, wet, and superheated steam. Also, the advantages of hot air, hot water, and absorption cooling can be achieved at the same time. Here, solar irradiation is the heating source, hence the system is known as a trigeneration solar thermal system (TST). Normally, a trigeneration system comprises of a photovoltaic thermal (PVT) system, solar thermal systems, and solar photovoltaic cells for the purpose of co-generation of thermal and electrical power from solar energy [31]. Working temperature, energy performance, and system operating mode depend on the removal of hot fluid, the type of collector installed, the system’s energy balance, and the solar fraction. Modelling and simulation are performed using Polysun 12.0.8 software, which was designed by Vela Solaris AG, Winterthur, Switzerland. This software proves to be a viable source for designing, simulation, and optimization of global energy systems used for commercial as well as residential buildings [31,32]. While designing a plan for an energy system, it is an important feature that the system should operate in unison with the requisite and also be cost-efficient. The end users of this software receive actual and well-planned energy systems because of its efficient computational core. Simulation purposes are served by almost 150,000 components which work on different characteristic curves for various solar modules. It also provides a detailed professional report for comparison and effective optimization in the same arena. Hence, the user can create energy systems to meet the various electrical demands along with maximum flexibility [33].

The software library is a hustle-free space where all the system components are well arranged to meet the user's demands. The wizard, present in the graphical user interface, provides initial starting data to create an appropriate climate model. It is actually a repository of free climate data for the simulation of off-grid buildings. In Polysun, a single-line energy-efficient building diagram can be easily developed for both the types of buildings, whether residential or commercial. Various essential features like electricity consumption, overview of solar thermal energy, system overview, and result investigation of different components, annual energy flow diagram, and heat pump running status were studied by the software [34,35]. The determination of results was performed annually. The trigeneration system unit is proposed by selecting device regulatory results obtained from the two metaheuristic algorithms. Hence, two vivid systems were designed to study the efficient working of the ZnO/CQDs cells.

Heat which is recovered at the concentrator plate is sent to the end-most area of the system, as well as the water running through the implanted pipes, remains at a very low temperature. The complete system has a concentrator reflectivity of about 70 %, which means 30 % of the incident solar irradiation heat is retrieved and it is used for enhancement of the system in order to reduce weight and volume, along with a reduction in cost. The heat so recovered can be utilized in drying numerous items besides the usual heat accumulation [36,37]. This system focuses on storing heat to maintain a continuous supply even late at night. Also, when it is cloudy, the heat dissipation can be used to meet the need for different temperature ranges. The proposed system is a sustainable alternative that is useful in remote areas as well.

The suggested trigeneration systems were tested for a typical meteorological year (TMY) under Asian climatic data. The authentic simulated setup for ZnO/CQDs cells optimised with GA and PSO is shown in Figs. 16(a), 16(b)
. The outlined system consists of all the necessary modules that furnish results against which developments in examination can be assessed. Tables 12 and 13
showcase the detailed analysis of the PV/T and PV module data for the ZnO/CQDs system optimised by GA and PSO, respectively. Also, the total flow of energy from both the simulated models is shown in Fig. 17(a), (b)
.

Haldia, a small town in West Bengal, is situated at a longitude of 88.01° and latitude of 21.94°. The climate is generally a tropical savanna climate. Here, temperatures in the cold season vary from 7 °C to 22 °C. According to barometrical statics, the outdoor temperature is 26.8 °C.The statics for annual global irradiation and annual diffuse irradiation were 1,727 kWh/m2 and 897 kWh/m2 respectively. Simulations were conducted on the North Service Centre of Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, West Bengal, which is an official building for 40 occupants consisting of 2 floors with a heated/air-conditioned living area of 3,300 m2 [38].

The hybrid PV module results obtained by GA and PSO are used for the simulation probe. Both the algorithmic approaches were 20 in number, with a collector area of 63.6 m2 and 61.6 m2 respectively. They also used tube collectors and solo sheets whose total produced electricity was 24,334 kWh and 24,928 kWh. The maximum value of energy savings was 26,177.9 kWh and 27,412.5 kWh respectively. The ZnO/CQDs solar DHW system formulated by GA results was designed with 63.6 m2 of PV/T collector area. The in streaming temperature was 40.4 °C and the outlet temperature was 36 °C while the machine was running [39]. Considering the HVAC system, a HP unit with a sufficient power rating was chosen to process hot and cold water, respectively [40,41]. For simulation of terminal HVAC units, proper selection of fan-coils has been made [42,43]. The installed PV modules and the slope are solely user defined [44,45]. Depending on the obtained results, Table 14 gives details on the yearly demand and production for the two proposed trigeneration systems. Tables 12 and 13 show an analogous study between the two systems, and it was concluded that the ZnO/CQDs system using PSO is superior to the ZnO/CQDs system using GA. Abiding by the energy flow presentations in Fig. 17(a), (b), it is evident that the heat pump for the module formulated by the GA unit operates at 73.42 % and the heat pump for the module formulated by the PSO unit has an efficiency of 75.02 %. Also, Table 14 gives a detailed description of the energy demanded and produced annually by the two presented solar trigeneration models.

The trigeneration system of the PSO optimised module unit works out efficiently, approaching a percentage of solar thermal energy of 80.1 %. The solar fraction of the building is 82.4 % and the solar fraction of hot water is 83.8 %. For the system formulated by GA, the percentage of solar thermal energy was 81 %; the values for the solar fraction of the building were 77.9 % and the solar fraction of hot water was 77.7 %. Both DC and AC electricity were generated, which were 6,829 kWh and 5,863 kWh, respectively. Hence, it is observed that trigeneration systems based on results obtained by the PSO algorithm work more efficiently than the ones based on GA metrics.

The basic goal of this research was to create a predictive model that could determine the requisite photovoltaic characteristics of a ZnO/CQDs hybrid solar cell. In this context, a new estimating approach utilizing GA and PSO optimization was presented. To validate the suggested concept, simulation-based hybrid photovoltaic experiments were carried out. The preliminary findings were compared with highly efficient optimization techniques, especially ANN-FA, to demonstrate the model's reliability. Overall, the comparison revealed that, while all three optimize models are capable of delivering a fairly precise assessment of the output parameters, the PSO model has the finest RMSE values. After testing all conceivable networks with empirical observations, it was determined that the PSO model with 100 population size was the optimum and version no. 7 (C1 = 1.42 and C2 = 2.37) outperforms the other models. Moreover, it is worthy of note that the findings demonstrate the deviation range (0.000461–0.0453) of the predicted values generated from the proposed method for the ZnO/CQDs cell to be smaller than that of the GA (0.003674–0.1128) and ANN-FA (0.000344–0.11321) for the same. Furthermore, the variation interval of the silicon-based cell employing PSO to predict the output was (0.0028–0.0472), which gives superior results than GA (0.003717–0.01209) and the ANN-FA (0.003474–0.0102954). Finally, the finest PSO model study proved the superiority of the proposed technique. Also, to substantiate the application arena, a simulation platform, Polysun 12.0.8, is used to simulate and analyze the performance of hybrid cells optimized by GA and PSO. It is observed that the cell optimized using Particle Swarm Optimization obtains 7.85 % higher efficacy than the one optimized using Genetic Algorithm. In the Polysun 12.0.8 environment, the domestic load profile is selected using traditional system results. Further, it is noticed that both the proposed system models substantiate comprehensive theories regarding the imperfections in the application domain of solar advancements. Suppose, if the necessity rises in low irradiation seasons, then these simulated units help in predicting the energy utilization as well as the efficiency during cold and windy weather. This work also suggests the utmost requirement of seasonal energy storage systems and alternative energy source models during winter and windy seasons.

Subsequent studies in the future will focus on improving the efficacy of the metrics identification and optimization methods based on other metaheuristic approaches in varied atmospheric situations (solar radiation and temperature). Furthermore, organizing intensive cost assumptions will be critical to avoid unprofitable events, making verification of frugal system designs possible.

Sakshi Tyagi: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation. Pawan Kumar Singh: Data curation, Supervision. Arun Kumar Tiwari: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their special gratitude to Mr. Shobhit Singh, Deputy Manager, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, Purba Medinipur, West Bengal, India for his extensive help in performing our study on solar trigeneration system at the North service centre building of HPL, India. Also, we would like to acknowledge Dr. Radha Das, Professor and Dean, Department of Chemical Engineering, Haldia Institute of Technology and Dr. Rabindra N. Jana, Associate Professor, Department of Chemical Engineering, Haldia Institute of Technology, for their constant support provided during the experimental research phase.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para:
The parameters observed during the GA calculation:
•
Population condition
Population Size: 50 (As the input variables are less than 5)
Population Type: Double vector
Initial Scores: NULL
Initial Population: NULL
Creation Function: Constraint dependent
• Selection condition
Selection Function: Tournament
Tournament Size: 2
• Reproduction condition
Crossover Fraction: 0.8
• Mutation
Mutation Function: dependent on constant
• Crossover
Crossover Function: Intermediate
Fraction: 0.2
Interval: 20
Direction: Forward
Ratio: 1
Migration
Hybrid function
Hybrid Function: None
Multiobjective problem settings
Distance Measure Function: @ distance crowding
Pareto Front Population Fraction: 0.35
Stall Time Limit: Infinity
Function Tolerance: 1x10-4
Fitness Limit: Infinity
Stall Generations: 100
Stopping Criteria
Generations: 100 × number of variables
Time Limit: Infinity
Constraint Tolerance: 1x10-3
• Display to command window
Level of Display: Off
• User function evaluation
Evaluation Fitness Function: In serial

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comprehensive Overview of Meta-Heuristic Algorithm Applications on PV Cell Parameter Identification
Energ Convers Manag

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, B., Wang, J., Zhang, X., Yu, T., Yao, W., Shu, H., et al., 2020, “Comprehensive Overview of Meta-Heuristic Algorithm Applications on PV Cell Parameter Identification”,Energ. Convers. Manag., 208 (15), pp. 112595 doi:10.1016/j.enconman.2020.112595.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of microchannel-porous media and nanofluid on temperature and performance of CPV system
J. Therm. Anal. Calorim.
